oai_dc:dc.dc:title: What About Helmeted Guinea Fowl?
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Howland, Zoe
oai_dc:dc.dc:description:
Guinea fowl—awesome birds! At least that has been my experience over the past 30 years. They have some extraordinary qualities that really endear them to people. They are excellent for organic insect control, if that’s your goal. Guinea fowl devour fleas, ticks, grasshoppers, chiggers, and a variety of other troublesome insects. As for snakes—most tend to stay away! Eggs Guineas lay seasonally, which means you don’t have to gather eggs during the cold winter months. Their eggs are higher in protein and lower in cholesterol than chicken eggs, making them a healthier option. In many cultures, guinea eggs are considered a delicacy. I tend to think of them more as a commodity, since I use them to hatch keets (baby guineas) and sell them for a modest sum. Watchdogs Guineas make excellent watchdogs. They will sound an earsplitting alarm whenever anything unfamiliar enters your property.
Beauty Their natural feather pattern—gray with white speckling—provides excellent camouflage, making them difficult to spot when hiding from predators or sitting on a well-hidden nest out in the pasture. They also come in a variety of other colors, adding even more visual interest. Sounds like the perfect bird, right? Not so fast. Guineas are not like chickens—they are far less domesticated and come with their own unique challenges.
[For the full article and image, please continue to the PDF]
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 52 No. 2 (2025): AFA Watchbird Fall/Winter 2025; 34-36
